skill.name: omarchy-theming
skill.description: Create, validate, and troubleshoot Omarchy desktop themes using colors.toml palettes, theme directory structure, and application-specific template guidance.
skill.summary: Build Omarchy themes that apply consistent colors across terminals, Hyprland, Waybar, btop, browsers, and desktop apps.
skill.author: BitYoungjae

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk keywords, but review found they are mostly false positives from color variables, hex color values, theme file names, and Markdown examples. The skill does not contain executable code, prompt injection, credential handling, or automatic network behavior. It does provide user-run shell commands that create files under ~/.config/omarchy and one sudo package-install troubleshooting command, so publication is acceptable with a medium-risk warning.

medium_findings.title: User-Run Shell Commands Modify Desktop Configuration
medium_findings.description: The skill includes shell examples for creating theme directories, applying themes, managing themes, and creating a light.mode marker under ~/.config/omarchy. These commands are legitimate for an Omarchy theming skill, but they can modify local desktop configuration when a user or agent executes them.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible Markdown examples, not hidden execution paths. Risk is still real if an agent runs them without confirmation because they change files in the user home directory and apply desktop themes.

medium_findings.title: Sudo Package Installation Command In Troubleshooting Guidance
medium_findings.description: The troubleshooting section suggests running sudo pacman -S yq. This is a legitimate dependency installation command, but it requires elevated privileges and can change system packages if executed.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The sudo command is explicit and requires privileged package installation. It appears benign and contextual, but users should approve it before execution.

low_findings.title: Static High-Risk Keyword Matches Are Theme Color False Positives
low_findings.description: Static C2 and weak-cryptography alerts map to words such as cpu_box, cpu_end, colors.toml, color1, color13, and hex color values in theme examples. No command-and-control behavior, cryptographic function, or encoded payload was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line review shows theme variable names and palette values, not network endpoints or cryptographic algorithms. The matches are scanner keyword collisions with desktop theme content.

low_findings.title: Hidden Home Directory Access Is Expected Theme Storage
low_findings.description: The references to ~/.config/omarchy point to the normal Omarchy theme directory and a light.mode marker. No evidence was found of reading secrets, enumerating unrelated hidden files, or exfiltrating local data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The hidden path is a documented configuration directory used for Omarchy themes. The access pattern is limited to creating theme assets and markers.

dangerous_patterns.title: Manual Privileged Package Installation
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documents sudo pacman -S yq for resolving yq errors. This should remain user-confirmed and should not be run automatically by an AI agent.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The privileged command is directly present, but it is troubleshooting documentation rather than covert execution.

content.value_statement: Building Omarchy themes requires matching color variables across many desktop applications. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code practical rules, examples, and checks for complete theme creation.
actual_capabilities: Defines the required 22 colors.toml variables for Omarchy themes.
Explains theme directory structure, including backgrounds, preview files, and light.mode.
Maps color variables to Alacritty, Kitty, Ghostty, Hyprland, Waybar, btop, Obsidian, Chromium, and related templates.
Provides example dark and light palettes for common theme styles.
Lists Omarchy theme commands for applying, listing, installing, removing, updating, and rotating backgrounds.
Gives troubleshooting checks for missing colors, invalid hex values, and missing yq.
limitations: It does not include executable automation scripts or validators.
It assumes the user already has Omarchy and the theme commands installed.
It cannot verify wallpaper files, contrast quality, or installed application themes by itself.
It includes shell commands that should be reviewed before execution.

use_cases.title: Create a New Theme Palette
use_cases.description: Generate a complete colors.toml palette with all required variables and supporting theme file recommendations.

use_cases.title: Port an Existing Color Scheme
use_cases.description: Translate a known palette into Omarchy variables and map it across terminal, Hyprland, Waybar, and app templates.

use_cases.title: Troubleshoot Theme Integration
use_cases.description: Check missing variables, invalid color formats, light mode markers, and app-specific template behavior.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Basic Theme
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a complete Omarchy colors.toml for a calm dark theme. Include all 22 required variables and explain any light or dark mode assumptions.

prompt_templates.title: Convert a Palette
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert this color palette into an Omarchy theme. Map each color to accent, foreground, background, selection colors, and ANSI color0 through color15.

prompt_templates.title: Review Theme Completeness
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this Omarchy theme directory plan. Identify missing files, missing colors.toml variables, invalid hex formats, and app-specific files I should add.

prompt_templates.title: Design Full App Coverage
prompt_templates.prompt: Design an Omarchy theme strategy that covers terminals, Hyprland, Waybar, btop, Obsidian, Chromium, Neovim, and VS Code using one coherent palette.

output_examples.input: User asks for a dark blue Omarchy theme.
output_examples.output: A complete palette plan with all required core UI colors, ANSI normal colors, ANSI bright colors, and notes for backgrounds and preview files.

output_examples.input: User provides a theme that does not apply correctly.
output: The user receives targeted checks for colors.toml, light.mode, and omarchy-theme-set.

output_examples.input: User wants one palette to work across terminal and desktop apps.
output_examples.output: A mapping explains which variables affect terminal cursors, selections, Hyprland borders, Waybar CSS, btop graphs, and browser theme color.

best_practices: Define all 22 colors.toml variables before testing a theme.
Use #RRGGBB hex values consistently and reserve light.mode for light themes.
Review shell commands before running them because they can modify desktop configuration.
anti_patterns: Do not omit backgrounds because Omarchy expects a backgrounds directory for themes.
Do not mix RGB, HSL, named colors, and hex values in colors.toml.
Do not run package installation or theme installation commands without user approval.

faq.question: What does this skill help create?
faq.answer: It helps create Omarchy desktop themes with complete colors.toml palettes and related theme files.

faq.question: Does it generate application-specific theme files?
faq.answer: It explains how variables map to many application templates, but it does not include an automated generator script.

faq.question: How many color variables are required?
faq.answer: Omarchy themes require 22 variables: 6 core UI values and 16 ANSI colors.

faq.question: Can it help with light themes?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents light palettes and the empty light.mode marker file used for light themes.

faq.question: Is this skill safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes, with a medium warning. It is documentation-based, but it includes shell commands that can modify local configuration.

faq.question: Which AI tools can use it?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
